title: Two continuity validators enforce different relation-integrity guarantees
summary: The aggregate validator under tools/continuity validates required fields, enumerated values, unique IDs, relation target existence, self-reference, and the requirement that a superseded claim identify a target. The later relation validator under operations/continuity additionally rejects duplicate relation targets and supersession cycles, but accepts only an already-aggregated index object and is not shown as part of the aggregate repository gate. The durable state is therefore not simply 'continuity relations are validated': two partially overlapping contracts exist, and the repository-wide path may not enforce the stricter cycle and duplicate-edge guarantees until the implementations are composed or unified.
contradicts: Any current-state claim that one repository-wide continuity validator demonstrably enforces every relation guarantee implemented by both validator modules

status_relations.observed: The two modules have overlapping but non-identical acceptance rules and different input interfaces.
status_relations.inferred: A record set can satisfy the aggregate validator while still containing a duplicate relation target or a supersession cycle rejected by the newer relation validator.
status_relations.proposed: Compose the stricter relation validator into the aggregate repository gate, then retain one capability matrix and adversarial suite as the authoritative contract.
status_relations.superseded: Treating the existence of either validator alone as proof that all continuity relation invariants are enforced repository-wide.
status_relations.unresolved: No completed CI run or source integration observed in this run proves that the stricter relation validator executes against the aggregated monolithic and additive record set.

falsification_or_review_route: Fetch both modules at the recorded blob SHAs. Falsify this record by showing that the aggregate repository gate imports or equivalently implements duplicate-target and supersession-cycle rejection over the complete monolithic-plus-additive record set, or by demonstrating that the two validators have identical accepted and rejected input domains. Structural validation does not establish truth of any continuity claim.
